skill.name: medchem
skill.description: Apply medicinal chemistry rules, structural alerts, PAINS filters, and complexity metrics to prioritize compound libraries.
skill.summary: Screen and prioritize molecules with medicinal chemistry filters and batch reporting.
skill.author: K-Dense-AI

security_audit.summary: AI review found the high static score is driven by Markdown code examples and medicinal chemistry terminology, not malicious behavior. The bundled Python script performs expected local file reads and writes for batch filtering. No prompt injection, hidden network exfiltration, subprocess execution, or credential handling was found.

low_findings.title: Static Findings Are Documentation False Positives
low_findings.description: The external command findings occur in Markdown code fences and inline API examples. The weak cryptography and C2 keyword alerts match chemistry terms such as CNS, PAINS, and warhead, not executable cryptographic or command behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are Markdown examples or medicinal chemistry catalog text. I found no shell execution, cryptographic operation, or command-and-control logic in those contexts.

low_findings.title: Local File Processing Script
low_findings.description: The bundled Python script reads user-supplied molecule files and writes CSV and summary outputs. This is expected for batch filtering, but users should choose trusted input files and intended output paths.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The script clearly performs local file reads and writes based on command-line paths. The behavior is legitimate for the stated workflow and shows no exfiltration or hidden execution.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Documentation Links
low_findings.description: The network findings are visible documentation links to the medchem documentation and repository. No executable network request or automatic data transmission was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are plain Markdown URLs. A broader search found no requests, sockets, curl, wget, webhook, token, or secret handling behavior.

content.value_statement: Compound triage can be slow and inconsistent across discovery teams. This skill applies medicinal chemistry rules, alerts, and reports for repeatable prioritization.
actual_capabilities: Applies Lipinski, Veber, CNS, lead-like, and related drug-likeness rules.
Screens molecules with PAINS, NIBR, common alert, and Lilly demerit filters.
Detects chemical groups, reactive groups, hinge binders, and custom SMARTS patterns.
Calculates molecular complexity with Bertz, Whitlock, and Barone methods.
Provides a batch script for CSV, TSV, SDF, and SMILES text inputs.
Generates filtered result tables and plain text summary reports.
limitations: Filter results are guidelines and do not replace medicinal chemistry review.
The script requires medchem, datamol, pandas, RDKit, and tqdm dependencies.
Results depend on input molecule quality and valid SMILES or SDF records.
The skill does not perform docking, synthesis planning, or biological assay analysis.

use_cases.title: Screen a Discovery Library
use_cases.description: Apply broad drug-likeness and alert filters before selecting compounds for assays.

use_cases.title: Prioritize Lead Series
use_cases.description: Compare candidate molecules against stricter lead-like rules and complexity thresholds.

use_cases.title: Prepare Reproducible Reports
use_cases.description: Run batch filtering and produce summary files that document rule outcomes.

prompt_templates.title: Explain Basic Filters
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of a compound list.
prompt_templates.prompt: Explain which medchem filters I should apply to a small oral drug discovery library. Keep the recommendations practical.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Screening Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a workflow using Rule of Five, Veber, PAINS, and common alerts for my CSV compound library.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Lead Candidates
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me compare lead candidates using lead-like strict rules, NIBR filters, Lilly demerits, and Bertz complexity.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Batch Command Plan
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced execution planning with output controls.
prompt_templates.prompt: Plan a reproducible batch filtering run for an SDF file. Include inputs, filters, expected outputs, and review checks.

output_examples.input: A user asks how to screen a CSV file with SMILES for oral drug-like molecules.
output: Recommended filters: Rule of Five, Veber, PAINS, and common structural alerts.
Expected output: a result table with pass columns and a summary of rejection reasons.
Review note: inspect borderline compounds before removing them from a campaign.

output_examples.input: A user wants stricter criteria for lead optimization after initial screening.
output: Recommended filters: lead-like strict rules, NIBR filters, Lilly demerits, and complexity limits.
Expected output: prioritized candidates with clear pass and failure indicators.
Review note: keep compounds with known target-specific reasons for rule exceptions.

output_examples.input: A user needs to identify reactive or assay-interfering substructures.
output: Recommended checks: PAINS, common alerts, reactive groups, and custom SMARTS patterns.
Expected output: matched alert names and counts for each molecule.
Review note: confirm structural alerts with assay context and chemist review.

best_practices: Use multiple filters together and document why each filter was selected.
Review failures manually before removing valuable compounds from a project.
Save input files, filter settings, and summaries for reproducible decisions.
anti_patterns: Do not treat rule failures as automatic proof that a compound is unusable.
Do not mix unrelated project goals in one filtering threshold set.
Do not run batch scripts on untrusted files or unexpected output locations.

faq.question: Does this skill replace medicinal chemistry judgment?
faq.answer: No. It provides rule-based evidence that should be reviewed with target biology and project context.

faq.question: Which molecule formats does the batch script support?
faq.answer: The script supports CSV, TSV, SDF, and plain text files with one SMILES string per line.

faq.question: Can it apply PAINS and structural alert filters?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes PAINS, common alerts, NIBR filters, and Lilly demerit workflows.

faq.question: Can it calculate molecular complexity?
faq.answer: Yes. It describes Bertz, Whitlock, and Barone complexity methods for filtering and comparison.

faq.question: Does it send molecules to an external service?
faq.answer: No evidence found. The included script processes local files and writes local outputs.

faq.question: What dependencies are needed for the script?
faq.answer: The script expects medchem, datamol, pandas, RDKit, and tqdm to be installed.
